metaDescription: Bookkeeping and year-end accounts for UK companies and sole traders. Cloud books kept MTD-ready and Companies House accounts filed early on fixed monthly fees.
title: Bookkeeping and Year-End Accounts
intro: Tidy, real-time books and accurate year-end accounts, prepared and filed well ahead of deadline by your own named accountant.
highlights: Companies House and HMRC filing handled
Records kept for at least 6 years

sections.heading: Bookkeeping and year-end accounts, handled end to end
body: Good year-end accounts start with good bookkeeping. We keep your records up to date through the year in cloud software, reconcile your bank feeds and tidy the detail as we go, so there is no scramble when your year end arrives.

When it does, we prepare your statutory accounts, agree them with you and file with Companies House and HMRC on your behalf. Because the underlying numbers are already clean, your accounts are usually ready weeks ahead of the deadline rather than days.
bullets: Regular reconciliation of bank, card and payment feeds
Purchase and sales records kept in order
Year-end statutory accounts prepared and explained
Filing with both Companies House and HMRC

sections.heading: Companies House and HMRC deadlines we keep you ahead of
body: Limited companies have several fixed deadlines, and the penalties for missing them are automatic. We track every date for you and build the work backwards from it, so nothing is left to the last minute.
A quick note on first accounts: a brand-new company's first set is due 21 months after the date of incorporation, after which the usual nine-month deadline applies each year.
bullets: Annual accounts to Companies House: normally within 9 months of the accounting reference date
First accounts: within 21 months of incorporation
Company Tax Return (CT600) to HMRC: within 12 months of the year end
Corporation tax payable: 9 months and 1 day after the year end
Confirmation statement (CS01): at least once a year

sections.heading: The right reporting standard for a small company
body: Most owner-managed companies report under the standards written for smaller businesses, which keep the accounts proportionate and the public record concise. The right choice depends on your size and what you are comfortable putting on file.
We assess which framework fits, prepare your accounts to that standard, and use filleted or abridged options where appropriate to keep commercially sensitive detail off the public record.
bullets: FRS 105 for micro-entities
FRS 102 Section 1A for small companies
Filleted or abridged accounts where available

sections.heading: Cloud bookkeeping that stays MTD-ready
body: We run your books in market-leading cloud software — Xero, QuickBooks, Sage or FreeAgent — so your numbers are live, backed up and available wherever you are.
Keeping everything digital also means your records stay Making Tax Digital ready, so as MTD continues to expand you are already set up the way HMRC expects rather than playing catch-up later.

sections.heading: A modernising Companies House and your records
body: Companies House is being modernised under the Economic Crime and Corporate Transparency Act, with measures such as identity verification and a move towards software-only filing being phased in. We keep on top of these changes so your filings stay compliant as the rules bed in.

Underpinning all of it is good record-keeping. Business records generally need to be kept for at least six years, and keeping them in the cloud means they are organised, secure and easy to retrieve if HMRC ever asks.

sections.heading: Sole traders and the self-employed
body: You do not need to be a limited company to get the same care. If you are a sole trader, we keep your records in order through the year and prepare the accounts that feed straight into your Self Assessment return.
That means a clear picture of how the business is doing, accounts that stand up to scrutiny, and no last-minute panic when the tax return is due.

faqs.question: When are my limited company accounts due at Companies House?
faqs.answer: Private limited companies normally file their annual accounts within nine months of the accounting reference date. A company's very first accounts are different — they are due 21 months after the date of incorporation. We track both dates and file ahead of them.

faqs.question: What is the difference between my accounts and my Company Tax Return?
faqs.answer: They are two separate obligations. Your annual accounts go to Companies House, while your Company Tax Return (CT600) goes to HMRC within 12 months of your year end. Note that corporation tax itself is payable earlier — 9 months and 1 day after the year end.

faqs.question: How long do I need to keep my business records?
faqs.answer: Business records must generally be kept for at least six years. Keeping them in cloud accounting software makes this straightforward, as everything is stored securely, organised by year and easy to retrieve if HMRC requests it.

faqs.question: Do I have to put my full accounts on the public record?
faqs.answer: Not always. Many small companies and micro-entities report under FRS 102 Section 1A or FRS 105 and can use filleted or abridged accounts, which keep some commercially sensitive detail off the public file. We advise on the right approach for your business.

faqs.question: Can you take over the bookkeeping I am doing myself?
faqs.answer: Yes. We can take the whole thing off your hands or support you to keep doing the parts you enjoy. Either way we set up your cloud software, get the records clean and keep them MTD-ready throughout the year.

metaDescription: Corporation tax and Self Assessment planning for UK companies and directors. Proactive advice on dividends, allowances and deadlines on fixed monthly fees.
title: Tax Planning & Returns
intro: Forward-looking corporation tax and Self Assessment advice that keeps you compliant and helps you keep more of your profit — planned all year, not just filed at the deadline.
highlights: Corporation tax returns prepared and filed
Self Assessment for directors and the self-employed
Salary and dividend planning for shareholders
Marginal relief reviewed where profits fall in the band
Capital allowances and full expensing claimed
Pension contributions used as a planning lever

sections.heading: Corporation tax and Self Assessment, planned year-round
body: Filing a return is the easy part. The value is in the planning we do before your year end, when there is still time to act — reviewing how profit is taxed, how it is drawn and which allowances apply.

We look after the corporation tax for your company and the Self Assessment for you personally, joining the two up so decisions made in the business make sense for you as an individual too. This is general guidance rather than advice on your specific circumstances.

sections.heading: How corporation tax is charged in 2025/26
body: The rate your company pays depends on its profit. Companies with profits in the band between the two thresholds pay an effective marginal rate of around 26.5%, which is why managing the timing of income and costs can matter.
We calculate your position, apply marginal relief where it is due and make sure the return reflects every allowance you are entitled to.
bullets: Main rate of 25% on profits of £250,000 or more
Small profits rate of 19% on profits of £50,000 or less
Marginal relief between £50,000 and £250,000 (an effective marginal rate of around 26.5%)
Corporation tax payable 9 months and 1 day after the year end
CT600 filed within 12 months of the year end

sections.heading: Self Assessment deadlines and payments on account
body: If you are a director, landlord or sole trader, you will usually need to file a Self Assessment return. The deadlines are fixed and the penalties automatic, so we keep you ahead of every one.
Many people are caught out by payments on account — advance instalments towards next year's bill. We calculate these in advance so there are no surprises in January or July.
bullets: Register for Self Assessment by 5 October after the tax year
File online and pay by 31 January
Payments on account due 31 January and 31 July
The UK tax year runs 6 April to 5 April

sections.heading: Salary and dividend planning for director-shareholders
body: If you own and run your company, how you pay yourself makes a real difference. We model the mix of salary and dividends to suit your circumstances, factoring in your allowances and the relevant dividend tax rates.
In most cases a considered blend of salary and dividends is more efficient than either alone — but the right answer depends on your wider income, which is exactly what we work through with you.
bullets: Dividend allowance of £500
Dividend tax rates of 8.75% (basic), 33.75% (higher) and 39.35% (additional)
Personal allowance of £12,570, tapered above £100,000
Higher-rate threshold of £50,270

sections.heading: Allowances on equipment, vehicles and investment
body: If your business invests in equipment, the right capital allowances can give you valuable relief in the year you spend. We make sure qualifying purchases are claimed correctly and timed to your advantage.
For companies, full expensing offers a 100% first-year allowance on qualifying new plant and machinery, alongside the Annual Investment Allowance for a broader range of assets.
bullets: Annual Investment Allowance of £1,000,000
Full expensing: 100% first-year allowance on qualifying new plant and machinery (companies)

sections.heading: Pensions as a planning lever
body: Pension contributions are one of the most effective tools available to business owners, letting you extract profit tax-efficiently while building for the future.
We factor pension planning into your wider profit-extraction strategy so it works together with your salary, dividends and allowances rather than in isolation.

faqs.question: What rate of corporation tax will my company pay?
faqs.answer: It depends on your profit. The small profits rate of 19% applies to profits of £50,000 or less, the main rate of 25% applies to profits of £250,000 or more, and profits in between attract marginal relief — an effective marginal rate of around 26.5%.

faqs.question: When do I have to pay my corporation tax?
faqs.answer: Corporation tax is payable 9 months and 1 day after the end of your accounting period. That is earlier than the deadline for filing the return itself, which is 12 months after your year end, so it pays to plan the cash ahead of time.

faqs.question: What are payments on account for Self Assessment?
faqs.answer: Payments on account are advance instalments towards your next tax bill, usually due on 31 January and 31 July. They catch a lot of people out in their first year of self-employment, so we calculate them in advance and explain exactly what to expect.

faqs.question: Is it better to pay myself in salary or dividends?
faqs.answer: For most director-shareholders a blend of the two is more efficient than either on its own, taking account of the personal allowance, the £500 dividend allowance and the dividend tax rates. The right mix depends on your wider income, which we model with you.

faqs.question: Can my company claim tax relief when it buys equipment?
faqs.answer: Usually, yes. The Annual Investment Allowance gives relief on up to £1,000,000 of qualifying spend, and companies can also use full expensing — a 100% first-year allowance on qualifying new plant and machinery. We make sure purchases are claimed correctly and timed well.

metaTitle: VAT Returns & Making Tax Digital | MMR
metaDescription: VAT returns and Making Tax Digital handled end to end for UK businesses. The right scheme, accurate quarterly returns and no missed deadlines, on fixed fees.
title: VAT & Making Tax Digital
intro: MTD-compliant VAT handled from registration to filing — the right scheme for your business, accurate returns, and never a missed submission.
highlights: Scheme reviews to suit your business
Quarterly VAT returns prepared and filed
OSS and IOSS for EU B2C sales

sections.heading: VAT returns and Making Tax Digital, end to end
body: VAT is one of the easiest taxes to get wrong and one of the most costly when you do. We take care of the whole cycle — keeping the digital records, preparing each return, checking it and filing it on time.
Because your books are already in MTD-compatible software, the return is a by-product of bookkeeping that is already done, not a separate quarterly headache.

sections.heading: Do you need to register for VAT?
body: You must register once your taxable turnover crosses the threshold on a rolling 12-month basis, but in some cases registering voluntarily before that point makes sense. We monitor your turnover so you register at the right time, neither late nor unnecessarily early.
If your turnover falls, deregistration may be an option too, and we advise on whether it is worth it.
bullets: Registration threshold: £90,000 of taxable turnover (rolling 12 months) since 1 April 2024
Deregistration threshold: £88,000
Advice on voluntary registration where it benefits you

sections.heading: Getting the rates and the scheme right
body: Not everything you sell is necessarily taxed at the standard rate, and applying the wrong rate is a common and expensive mistake. We make sure each supply is treated correctly across the full range of VAT rates.
We also review which VAT scheme suits you. The right scheme can simplify your admin or improve your cash flow, and the best fit changes as a business grows.
bullets: Standard rate of 20%
Reduced rate of 5%
Zero-rated supplies at 0%
Exempt supplies
Standard, Flat Rate, Cash Accounting, Annual Accounting and retail or margin schemes

sections.heading: Making Tax Digital is mandatory for VAT
body: Making Tax Digital for VAT has applied to all VAT-registered businesses since April 2022. That means keeping digital records and filing through MTD-compatible software — paper records and manual submissions are no longer allowed.
We keep you fully compliant by running your VAT inside cloud software that meets HMRC's requirements, with a clear digital trail from transaction to return.

sections.heading: VAT return deadlines
body: Most businesses file VAT returns quarterly. The return and any payment are due one month and seven days after the period ends, and we make sure both are dealt with comfortably ahead of time.
We diarise every period for you, so there is no risk of a default and no last-minute rush to pull figures together.
bullets: VAT returns are usually quarterly
Return and payment due 1 month and 7 days after the period ends

sections.heading: Construction, imports and EU sales
body: Some sectors and transactions need special handling. If you work in construction, the VAT domestic reverse charge applies to most CIS services and changes how VAT is accounted for between contractors and subcontractors — we deal with this routinely.
We also handle the VAT side of trade beyond the UK, using postponed VAT accounting for imports and the OSS and IOSS schemes for EU business-to-consumer sales.
bullets: VAT domestic reverse charge for most construction (CIS) services
Postponed VAT accounting for imports
OSS and IOSS for EU B2C sales

faqs.question: When do I have to register for VAT?
faqs.answer: You must register once your taxable turnover exceeds £90,000 on a rolling 12-month basis, the threshold in place since 1 April 2024. Some businesses also choose to register voluntarily below that level. We monitor your turnover so you register at exactly the right time.

faqs.question: What does Making Tax Digital mean for my VAT?
faqs.answer: Making Tax Digital for VAT has been mandatory for all VAT-registered businesses since April 2022. You must keep digital records and file through MTD-compatible software. We run your VAT in cloud software that meets HMRC's requirements, keeping you fully compliant.

faqs.question: When are my VAT returns and payments due?
faqs.answer: VAT returns are usually filed quarterly, with the return and any payment due one month and seven days after the period ends. We diarise every period and prepare your return ahead of time so nothing is ever late.

faqs.question: Which VAT scheme is right for my business?
faqs.answer: It depends on your size, sector and cash flow. Options include the Standard, Flat Rate, Cash Accounting and Annual Accounting schemes, plus retail and margin schemes for some businesses. We review the choices and recommend the one that genuinely suits you.

faqs.question: How does VAT work for construction businesses?
faqs.answer: The VAT domestic reverse charge applies to most construction services within the CIS, which changes how VAT is accounted for between contractors and subcontractors rather than being charged in the usual way. We handle this for construction clients as a matter of routine.

metaDescription: Fully managed payroll services and auto-enrolment for UK employers. RTI submissions, payslips, P60s and pensions handled correctly every payday, on fixed fees.
title: Payroll & Pensions
intro: Fully managed RTI payroll and workplace pensions, so your team is paid correctly and on time every payday — and every submission to HMRC and your pension provider is handled for you.
highlights: RTI payroll run on or before each payday
FPS and EPS submissions to HMRC
Payslips, P60s, P45s and P11Ds produced
Declaration of compliance to The Pensions Regulator

sections.heading: Payroll services and auto-enrolment, fully managed
body: Payroll has to be right every single time, because your team notices immediately if it is not. We run your payroll each pay period, calculate the deductions, produce the payslips and report everything to HMRC on your behalf.
Alongside it we manage your workplace pension duties, so two of the most repetitive and rule-bound parts of running a business simply happen in the background.

sections.heading: Real Time Information reporting to HMRC
body: PAYE is operated in real time, which means HMRC expects to hear from you every time you pay your people, not just at year end. We make the required submissions on schedule, every period.
We send a Full Payment Submission on or before each payday and an Employer Payment Summary where one is needed, keeping your PAYE account accurate and up to date.
bullets: Full Payment Submission (FPS) on or before each payday
Employer Payment Summary (EPS) submitted where needed
Your PAYE position kept reconciled with HMRC

sections.heading: Workplace pensions and auto-enrolment
body: Every employer has automatic enrolment duties, and they do not stop once staff are enrolled. We assess your workforce, enrol those who qualify and keep the scheme running correctly.

Eligible jobholders are those aged 22 to State Pension age earning over £10,000. We manage contributions, the three-yearly re-enrolment cycle and the declaration of compliance to The Pensions Regulator.
bullets: Eligible jobholders: aged 22 to State Pension age earning over £10,000
Minimum total contribution of 8% of qualifying earnings, with the employer paying at least 3%
Qualifying earnings band for 2025/26: £6,240 to £50,270
Re-enrolment every three years
Declaration of compliance to The Pensions Regulator

sections.heading: Employer National Insurance and the Employment Allowance
body: Employing people carries an employer National Insurance cost, and getting the reliefs right keeps that cost in check. We calculate your employer NIC accurately each period.
Where your business is eligible we claim the Employment Allowance, which reduces your employer Class 1 National Insurance bill.
bullets: Employer (secondary) Class 1 NIC rate of 15% from 6 April 2025
Secondary threshold of £5,000
Employment Allowance of up to £10,500 from April 2025

sections.heading: Payslips, P60s and the statutory paperwork
body: There is a steady stream of documents an employer must produce, and your people have legal rights to some of them. We generate them all accurately and on time.
From the payslip every worker is entitled to, through to year-end and leaver paperwork and benefits reporting, it is all taken care of.
bullets: Payslips — a legal right for every worker
P60 at the end of each tax year
P45 for leavers
P11D for benefits in kind

sections.heading: Statutory pay and the National Living Wage
body: Sick pay, maternity pay and other statutory payments are fiddly to calculate and easy to get wrong. We administer statutory pay such as SSP and SMP straight through your payroll so the right amounts reach the right people.
We also help you stay on the right side of the National Minimum and Living Wage, which is reviewed each April, so your pay rates remain compliant as the rates change.

faqs.question: Which employees do I have to enrol in a workplace pension?
faqs.answer: Eligible jobholders — those aged 22 to State Pension age earning over £10,000 — must be automatically enrolled. The minimum total contribution is 8% of qualifying earnings, of which the employer pays at least 3%. We assess your team and manage the whole process.

faqs.question: Can I reduce my employer National Insurance bill?
faqs.answer: Often, yes. Many businesses can claim the Employment Allowance, worth up to £10,500 from April 2025, which reduces your employer Class 1 National Insurance. We check your eligibility and apply the claim through your payroll automatically.

faqs.question: What payroll documents do I need to give my staff?
faqs.answer: Every worker has a legal right to a payslip. You also need to provide a P60 after each tax year, a P45 when someone leaves, and a P11D where benefits in kind apply. We produce all of these accurately and on time as part of the service.

faqs.question: Do you handle statutory sick and maternity pay?
faqs.answer: Yes. Statutory payments such as SSP and SMP are administered through your payroll, so they are calculated correctly and paid through the normal pay run. We also help you keep pay rates in line with the National Living Wage, which is reviewed each April.

faqs.question: test faq
faqs.answer: test faq

metaDescription: Company formation and company secretarial services in the UK. Same-day incorporation, registered office and confirmation statements, kept fully compliant.
title: Company Formation & Secretarial
intro: Start right and stay compliant — fast incorporation at Companies House, the right structure for your plans, and ongoing company secretarial work handled on your behalf.
highlights: Guidance on the right business structure
Registered office and registered email set up
PSC register and statutory registers maintained
Corporation Tax, PAYE and VAT registrations
Support with identity verification under ECCTA

sections.heading: Company formation and company secretarial, done properly
body: Getting your company set up correctly from day one saves a great deal of correction later. We incorporate your company at Companies House — usually same day online — with the details and documents arranged the right way.
From there we look after the ongoing secretarial obligations that come with running a limited company, so the statutory side is never something you have to remember.

sections.heading: Choosing the right structure first
body: A limited company is not automatically the best vehicle for everyone, and the right structure depends on your plans, your risk and how you want to be taxed. Before we form anything, we help you choose with your eyes open.
We talk you through the trade-offs between operating as a sole trader, a limited company or a partnership or LLP, so the structure fits the business you actually intend to build.
bullets: Sole trader
Limited company
Partnership or LLP

sections.heading: What goes into incorporating a company
body: Incorporation involves more than just a name. There are several decisions and details to settle, and the rules on some of them have tightened recently.

Note that your registered office must now be an appropriate address — a PO box accommodation address is no longer acceptable — and a registered email address has been required since March 2024. We make sure each element is correct from the outset.
bullets: Company name
Registered office at an appropriate address (no PO box accommodation addresses)
Registered email address (required since March 2024)
SIC code
Directors and shareholders
Share capital
Articles of association (often model articles)

sections.heading: Statutory registers and the confirmation statement
body: Once a company exists, the law requires it to keep certain records and to confirm its details to Companies House each year. We maintain these for you so they are always accurate and up to date.
That includes keeping your People with Significant Control register and your other statutory registers in order, and filing your annual confirmation statement on time.
bullets: People with Significant Control (PSC) register maintained
Statutory registers kept up to date
Annual confirmation statement filed

sections.heading: Identity verification under the new rules
body: The Economic Crime and Corporate Transparency Act is introducing identity verification for directors and people with significant control as part of a wider effort to improve the reliability of the public register.
We keep across how and when these requirements apply, and help your directors and PSCs meet them, so your company stays compliant as the regime is rolled out.

sections.heading: Registering for the right taxes
body: A new company usually has tax registrations to deal with soon after it is formed, and missing them creates problems quickly. We handle the registrations that apply to you.
New companies register for Corporation Tax, and we add PAYE and VAT registrations where they are relevant — so you are set up correctly with HMRC from the start, not scrambling later.
bullets: Corporation Tax registration
PAYE registration where you employ staff
VAT registration where relevant

faqs.question: How long does it take to form a limited company?
faqs.answer: Incorporation at Companies House is usually completed the same day when done online. We gather the details, prepare the documents correctly and register the company for you, then deal with the Corporation Tax registration that follows.

faqs.question: Should I trade as a sole trader or a limited company?
faqs.answer: It depends on your plans, your appetite for risk and how you want to be taxed. A limited company offers limited liability and can be more tax-efficient at certain profit levels, but it carries more admin. We talk through the options before you commit.

faqs.question: Can I use my home or a PO box as my registered office?
faqs.answer: Your registered office must now be an appropriate address where documents can be acknowledged, and PO box accommodation addresses are no longer acceptable. A registered email address is also required. We make sure your details meet the current rules.

faqs.question: What is the confirmation statement and how often is it due?
faqs.answer: The confirmation statement confirms your company's key details to Companies House and must be filed at least once a year. We maintain your statutory registers, including the PSC register, and file the statement on time on your behalf.

faqs.question: What is changing with identity verification at Companies House?
faqs.answer: Under the Economic Crime and Corporate Transparency Act, identity verification is being introduced for directors and people with significant control. We keep across how the requirements apply and help your directors and PSCs meet them as the rules take effect.

metaTitle: Business Advisory & Cash Flow Forecasting | MMR
metaDescription: Business advisory and cash flow forecasting for ambitious UK businesses. Management accounts, virtual FD support, budgeting and funding readiness on fixed fees.
title: Business Advisory & Growth
intro: Numbers that drive decisions, not just compliance — management accounts, cash-flow forecasting and a dedicated adviser who helps you grow with confidence.
highlights: Monthly or quarterly management accounts and KPIs

sections.heading: Business advisory and cash flow forecasting that earns its keep
body: Compliance tells you what already happened. Advisory is about what happens next — using your numbers to make better decisions while there is still time to influence the result.
We work alongside you with regular management information and a forward view of your cash, so you are running the business on real data rather than gut feel and the latest bank balance.

sections.heading: Management accounts and the numbers that matter
body: A set of year-end accounts arrives too late to change anything. Management accounts give you the picture while it is still useful, focused on the handful of measures that actually move your business.
We produce them monthly or quarterly and talk you through what they mean, agreeing the KPIs that matter most to you so every report answers a question you actually care about.
bullets: Monthly or quarterly management accounts
KPIs tailored to your business
A plain-English review, not just a spreadsheet

sections.heading: Cash flow, budgets and planning for what-ifs
body: Profitable businesses still fail when they run out of cash. We build a forward view of yours so you can see what is coming and act before a squeeze becomes a problem.
Pairing a budget with scenario planning lets you stress-test the big decisions — a new hire, a price change, a quiet quarter — before you commit to them.
bullets: Cash-flow forecasting
Budgeting and target-setting
Scenario planning for the big decisions

sections.heading: A finance director, without the full-time cost
body: Plenty of growing businesses need finance leadership but are not ready to employ a finance director. Our virtual and outsourced FD support gives you that senior input when you need it.
It means a seasoned head for the big calls — pricing, investment, funding and strategy — on terms that suit a business at your stage.

sections.heading: Funding, investment and growth decisions
body: Whether you are approaching a lender or an investor, the quality of your numbers shapes the outcome. We help you get ready and put your case in the best possible light.

From business plans and forecasts to weighing up capital investment using allowances such as the Annual Investment Allowance and full expensing, we help you make the case and the call with confidence.
Where it looks relevant, we will also flag R&D tax relief so a potential claim is not left on the table.
bullets: Funding and investor readiness
Business plans and forecasts
Capital investment decisions using the Annual Investment Allowance (£1,000,000) and full expensing
Signposting R&D tax relief where relevant

sections.heading: Pricing, margins and getting paid properly
body: Small changes to pricing and margin often do more for profit than chasing extra sales. We dig into where you actually make money and where you quietly lose it.
Through benchmarking, pricing reviews and margin analysis — alongside profit-extraction and remuneration planning — we help you keep more of the value you create.
bullets: Benchmarking against your sector
Pricing and margin analysis
Profit-extraction and remuneration planning

faqs.question: What is the difference between management accounts and year-end accounts?
faqs.answer: Year-end accounts look backwards and are produced for compliance, often months after the event. Management accounts are produced monthly or quarterly to give you a timely, decision-ready view of performance, focused on the KPIs that matter most to your business.

faqs.question: Why does cash flow forecasting matter if my business is profitable?
faqs.answer: Profit and cash are not the same thing, and profitable businesses can still run short of cash. A forward-looking cash-flow forecast shows what is coming so you can plan around tax payments, hires and quieter periods before they cause a problem.

faqs.question: What does a virtual finance director actually do?
faqs.answer: A virtual FD gives you senior finance input without the cost of a full-time hire. That typically means help with the bigger decisions — pricing, budgeting, funding, investment and strategy — supported by the regular management information we prepare for you.

faqs.question: Can you help us get ready to raise funding?
faqs.answer: Yes. We help with the numbers lenders and investors expect to see, including business plans, forecasts and clear management accounts. We also help you weigh investment decisions and, where relevant, signpost reliefs such as R&D tax relief.

faqs.question: How do you help improve our profit?
faqs.answer: We look at where you genuinely make and lose money through benchmarking, pricing reviews and margin analysis, then combine that with profit-extraction and remuneration planning. The aim is to help you keep more of the value the business creates.
